skill.name: gpt-image-edit
skill.description: Edit images with OpenAI GPT Image 2 (the `/edit` endpoint of ChatGPT Images 2.0) on RunComfy — bundled with the model's documented prompting patterns so the skill gets sharper output than naive prompting against the same model. Documents GPT Image Edit's strengths (preservation language, multilingual in-image text editing, multi-reference up to 10 images, layout / typography precision), the schema, and when to route to Nano Banana Edit / Flux Kontext / GPT Image 2 t2i instead. Calls `runcomfy run openai/gpt-image-2/edit` through the local RunComfy CLI. Triggers on "gpt image edit", "gpt-image-edit", "chatgpt image edit", "edit with gpt image 2", or any explicit ask to edit with this model.
skill.summary: Edit images with OpenAI GPT Image 2 on RunComfy for multilingual text editing, identity preservation, and layout-precise modifications
skill.author: agentspace-so

security_audit.summary: Static analysis detected 79 potential security patterns (external_commands, network, filesystem). After semantic evaluation, all findings are FALSE_POSITIVES. The skill is a CLI wrapper for a legitimate image editing API service. External commands are hardcoded CLI invocations, network activity targets known RunComfy endpoints, and filesystem references are user-provided output directories. No prompt injection detected. Security documentation is comprehensive.

medium_findings.title: CLI Command Invocation Pattern
medium_findings.description: The skill invokes the RunComfy CLI (`runcomfy run openai/gpt-image-2/edit`) which executes external commands. This is by design for a CLI wrapper skill.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: CLI commands are hardcoded in documentation, not derived from user input. The skill documents legitimate API access patterns with no injection vectors.

medium_findings.title: Network Access to External Service
medium_findings.description: The skill makes HTTPS requests to RunComfy Model API endpoints (model-api.runcomfy.net).
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: All network targets are documented RunComfy service endpoints. No credential exfiltration or suspicious data transmission detected.

medium_findings.title: Filesystem Path References
medium_findings.description: The skill references filesystem paths for output directory specification and token storage.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Path references are user-provided output directories. Token storage location is documented legitimate RunComfy config path. No arbitrary file access beyond intended scope.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs in Documentation
low_findings.description: Documentation contains URLs to RunComfy service pages and documentation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Hardcoded URLs are standard documentation links. No dynamic URL construction from user input detected.

low_findings.title: Placeholder Image URLs in Examples
low_findings.description: Example code contains placeholder image URLs (e.g., 'https://.../portrait.jpg').
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Placeholder URLs in documentation examples are standard practice. No actual network requests to these placeholders.

content.user_title: Edit Images with GPT Image 2 on RunComfy
content.value_statement: Editing images with precise layout control and multilingual text is difficult with generic tools. This skill provides direct access to OpenAI GPT Image 2 edit endpoint with optimized prompting patterns for identity preservation and text editing.
actual_capabilities: Edit images while preserving identity (face, pose, clothing, brand elements)
Rewrite multilingual in-image text in Latin, kana, CJK, Cyrillic, and Arabic scripts
Compose images using multiple reference images (up to 10 sources)
Perform layout-precise edits with directional spatial language
Access GPT Image 2 through RunComfy CLI with documented prompting patterns
Automatic output download to user-specified directory
limitations: Size parameter accepts only 3 fixed values plus 'auto' (any other value returns 422 error)
Long compound prompts may cause output drift; recommended to split into multiple passes
Batch consistency across many images is better handled by Nano Banana Edit (up to 20 images)
Photorealism on portraits is not the strongest capability compared to Nano Banana Pro

use_cases.title: Multilingual Ad Localization
use_cases.description: Take a single source image asset and generate multiple language variants with the same headline in different scripts. Maintain layout and brand identity across all variants.

use_cases.title: Brand-Safe Content Updates
use_cases.description: Swap headlines or CTAs in existing marketing materials without affecting the photograph, layout, or brand mark. Ensure consistent visual identity through targeted edits.

use_cases.title: Subject-Environment Composition
use_cases.description: Place a subject from one photograph into a different scene while preserving the subject's identity and matching the lighting and color palette of the target environment.

prompt_templates.title: Simple Background Swap
prompt_templates.prompt: Keep the main person and face identity unchanged. Replace the background with [description of new background].
prompt_templates.scenario: When you need to change the setting while keeping the subject intact

prompt_templates.title: Multilingual Text Replacement
prompt_templates.prompt: Keep the photograph, layout, lighting, and brand mark exactly as in the input. Replace only the in-image headline. The new headline reads [exact text to display] in [script type], same position and font weight as before.
prompt_templates.scenario: When translating in-image text to a different language or script

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Reference Composition
prompt_templates.prompt: Compose [subject element] from image 1 into [environment/scene] from image 2. Match the [lighting/color palette/atmosphere] of image 2. Keep [subject identity details] from image 1 unchanged.
prompt_templates.scenario: When combining elements from multiple reference images

prompt_templates.title: Layout-Precise Repositioning
prompt_templates.prompt: Keep [list of elements to preserve] unchanged. Move the [element to relocate] from [original position] to [new position].
prompt_templates.scenario: When you need precise control over element placement

output_examples.input: A portrait photo with English text that needs the headline changed to Japanese
output_examples.output: The same portrait with the text now reading '今日のおすすめ' in Japanese kana, same position and font style, all other elements unchanged

output_examples.input: A product shot with one background that should show a different environment
output_examples.output: The same product with identity preserved but now placed against a studio sweep background, product appearance unmodified

output_examples.input: Two images: one person, one room - composite the person into the room
output_examples.output: The person placed in the room with lighting matched to the room's window light, subject's face and clothing unchanged

best_practices: Always lead prompts with preservation goals (what to keep unchanged) before stating the change
Quote exact text characters when specifying in-image text changes rather than paraphrasing
Use directional spatial language for layout edits: 'from top-right to bottom-center' instead of vague positioning
Number reference images in multi-ref prompts: 'subject from image 1, lighting from image 2'
anti_patterns: Compound edit instructions with multiple changes cause output drift; split into sequential passes
Missing preservation language lets the model subtly modify faces, brands, or framing
Paraphrasing in-image text instead of quoting exact characters results in different text output
Requesting size values outside the 3 fixed options plus 'auto' returns a 422 error

faq.question: What makes GPT Image Edit different from other image editing tools?
faq.answer: GPT Image Edit excels at identity preservation through edits and multilingual in-image text rewriting in any script. It handles up to 10 reference images and provides layout-precise modifications.

faq.question: How do I pass multiple reference images?
faq.answer: Pass up to 10 publicly-fetchable HTTPS URLs as the images array. The first image is the primary input; remaining images are auxiliary references. In your prompt, refer to them by number: image 1, image 2, etc.

faq.question: Why should I use 'auto' for the size parameter?
faq.answer: The 'auto' setting preserves your input image ratio. Only override it when your edit explicitly changes the framing, such as cropping a 16:9 image to 1:1.

faq.question: What happens if my prompt is too complex?
faq.answer: Long compound prompts with multiple changes increase drift in the output. For complex edits, split into multiple sequential passes instead of one comprehensive prompt.

faq.question: How do I edit text that's already in an image?
faq.answer: Quote the exact text characters you want to change. For example: 'the headline reads "コーヒー" in bold Japanese kana'. Do not paraphrase or describe the meaning—quote the characters.

faq.question: What should I do if the output does not match the input ratio?
faq.answer: Ensure you pass 'size: "auto"' in your API request. If you explicitly want a different ratio, use one of the three fixed sizes: 1024_1024, 1024_1536, or 1536_1024.
